front_text: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) refers to glucose intolerance that develops or is first recognized during pregnancy.
front_html: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) refers to {{c1::glucose intolerance}} that develops or is first recognized during {{c1::pregnancy}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), insulin resistance arises in the second half of pregnancy due to placental hormones including human placental lactogen, estrogen, and progesterone.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), insulin resistance arises in the {{c1::second half}} of pregnancy due to placental hormones including {{c1::human placental lactogen}}, {{c1::estrogen}}, and {{c1::progesterone}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: Most patients with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) are asymptomatic, making routine screening essential.
front_html: Most patients with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) are {{c1::asymptomatic}}, making routine screening essential.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: Women who develop gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) are at higher risk for developing type 2 diabetes later in life.
front_html: Women who develop gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) are at higher risk for developing {{c1::type 2 diabetes}} later in life.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: All pregnant women should be screened for diabetes at their first prenatal visit to rule out preexisting type 2 diabetes. If initial testing is normal, repeat screening is performed at 24 to 28 weeks of gestation, when insulin resistance peaks.
front_html: All pregnant women should be screened for diabetes at their {{c1::first prenatal visit}} to rule out preexisting type 2 diabetes. If initial testing is normal, repeat screening is performed at {{c1::24 to 28}} weeks of gestation, when insulin resistance peaks.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: The first step in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) screening is a 50-gram 1-hour glucose challenge test, which is administered in a nonfasting state.
front_html: The first step in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) screening is a {{c1::50-gram}} {{c1::1-hour}} glucose challenge test, which is administered in a {{c1::nonfasting}} state.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: On the 50-gram 1-hour glucose challenge test, a blood glucose value of ≥130–140 mg/dL is considered abnormal and requires confirmation with a 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT).
front_html: On the 50-gram 1-hour glucose challenge test, a blood glucose value of {{c1::≥130–140}} mg/dL is considered abnormal and requires confirmation with a 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT).
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: The confirmatory 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) requires fasting for at least 8 hours overnight, followed by ingestion of 100 grams of glucose, with blood glucose measured at fasting, 1 hour, 2 hours, and 3 hours.
front_html: The confirmatory 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) requires fasting for at least {{c1::8}} hours overnight, followed by ingestion of {{c1::100}} grams of glucose, with blood glucose measured at fasting, 1 hour, 2 hours, and 3 hours.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is diagnosed on the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) when two or more of the diagnostic thresholds are met or exceeded.
front_html: Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is diagnosed on the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) when {{c1::two or more}} of the diagnostic thresholds are met or exceeded.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other thresholds: Fasting ≥95 mg/dL, 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, 2-hour ≥155 mg/dL, 3-hour ≥140 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the fasting threshold for diagnosis is ≥95 mg/dL.
front_html: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the fasting threshold for diagnosis is {{c1::≥95}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other diagnostic thresholds: 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, 2-hour ≥155 mg/dL, 3-hour ≥140 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 1-hour threshold for diagnosis is ≥180 mg/dL.
front_html: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 1-hour threshold for diagnosis is {{c1::≥180}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other diagnostic thresholds: Fasting ≥95 mg/dL, 2-hour ≥155 mg/dL, 3-hour ≥140 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 2-hour threshold for diagnosis is ≥155 mg/dL.
front_html: On the 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 2-hour threshold for diagnosis is {{c1::≥155}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other diagnostic thresholds: Fasting ≥95 mg/dL, 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, 3-hour ≥140 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: On the 3-hour oral gestational tolerance test (OGTT) for glucose diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 3-hour threshold for diagnosis is ≥140 mg/dL.
front_html: On the 3-hour oral gestational tolerance test (OGTT) for glucose diabetes mellitus (GDM), the 3-hour threshold for diagnosis is {{c1::≥140}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other diagnostic thresholds: Fasting ≥95 mg/dL, 1-hour ≥180 mg/dL, 2-hour ≥155 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: First-line management of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is non-pharmacologic and includes nutrition counseling, dietary modification, and regular exercise, along with daily self-monitoring of fasting and postprandial blood glucose.
front_html: First-line management of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is non-pharmacologic and includes {{c1::nutrition counseling}}, {{c1::dietary modification}}, and {{c1::regular exercise}}, along with daily self-monitoring of fasting and postprandial blood glucose.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for fasting levels is  mg/dL.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for fasting levels is {{c1::<95}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other glucose targets: 1-hour postprandial <140 mg/dL, 2-hour postprandial <120 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for 1-hour postprandial levels is  mg/dL.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for 1-hour postprandial levels is {{c1::<140}} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other glucose targets: Fasting <95 mg/dL, 2-hour postprandial <120 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for 2-hour postprandial levels is  mg/dL.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) management, the blood glucose target for 2-hour postprandial levels is {{c1::<120 }} mg/dL.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other glucose targets: Fasting <95 mg/dL, 1-hour postprandial <140 mg/dL
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: When lifestyle changes alone fail to achieve glucose targets in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the preferred pharmacologic treatment is insulin, because it does not cross the placenta and provides the most reliable glycemic control.
front_html: When lifestyle changes alone fail to achieve glucose targets in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), the preferred pharmacologic treatment is {{c1::insulin}}, because it does not {{c1::cross the placenta}} and provides the most reliable glycemic control.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), metformin may be used as an alternative pharmacologic agent for patients who cannot tolerate insulin or prefer oral therapy.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), {{c1::metformin}} may be used as an alternative pharmacologic agent for patients who cannot tolerate insulin or prefer oral therapy.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), patients with well-controlled disease can typically carry to term, but delivery at 38–39 weeks may be considered if glucose control is poor or if the fetus is large.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), patients with well-controlled disease can typically carry to term, but delivery at {{c1::38–39}} weeks may be considered if glucose control is poor or if the fetus is large.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), cesarean delivery may be recommended if the estimated fetal weight exceeds 4,500 grams, due to the risk of shoulder dystocia.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), cesarean delivery may be recommended if the estimated fetal weight exceeds {{c1::4,500}} grams, due to the risk of {{c1::shoulder dystocia}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) patients requiring insulin or with other risk factors, weekly non-stress tests (NSTs) are initiated around 32–34 weeks to assess fetal well-being.
front_html: In gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) patients requiring insulin or with other risk factors, weekly {{c1::non-stress tests (NSTs)}} are initiated around {{c1::32–34}} weeks to assess fetal well-being.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: The non-stress test (NST) monitors fetal heart rate and reactivity in response to fetal movements.
front_html: The non-stress test (NST) monitors {{c1::fetal heart rate}} and {{c1::reactivity}} in response to fetal movements.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: The most common complication of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is macrosomia (excessive fetal growth), which increases the risk of shoulder dystocia during delivery.
front_html: The most common complication of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is {{c1::macrosomia}} (excessive fetal growth), which increases the risk of {{c1::shoulder dystocia}} during delivery.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
extra: Other complications: neonatal hypoglycemia, respiratory distress syndrome, cardiac anomalies
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: Complications of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) include macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycemia, respiratory distress syndrome, and cardiac anomalies.
front_html: Complications of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) include macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, {{c1::neonatal hypoglycemia}}, {{c1::respiratory distress syndrome}}, and {{c1::cardiac anomalies}}.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: After delivery in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), insulin resistance typically resolves, but patients remain at significantly increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus in the future.
front_html: After delivery in gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), insulin resistance typically {{c1::resolves}}, but patients remain at significantly increased risk of developing {{c1::type 2 diabetes mellitus}} in the future.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: All women with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) should undergo postpartum diabetes screening using a 75-gram 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) at 4–12 weeks postpartum.
front_html: All women with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) should undergo postpartum diabetes screening using a {{c1::75-gram}} {{c1::2-hour}} oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) at {{c1::4–12}} weeks postpartum.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes

front_text: After gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), lifelong screening for type 2 diabetes should continue every 1–3 years, given the risk of recurrence in future pregnancies and later-life diabetes.
front_html: After gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), lifelong screening for type 2 diabetes should continue every {{c1::1–3}} years, given the risk of recurrence in future pregnancies and later-life diabetes.
tags: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health,Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20%,Gestational diabetes
curriculum_topic_path: Gynecologic, Sexual, and Reproductive Health > Prenatal Care/Pregnancy – 20% > Gestational diabetes
